writing_craft: In the chapter I want to develop the protagonist's backstory before the inciting incident.
In the manuscript I want to introduce the antagonist gradually rather than all at once.
In the screenplay I want to build tension through small visual details rather than dialogue.
In the novel I want to portray the city as a character with its own moods.
In the short story I want to capture a single moment of revelation in the closing scene.
In the play I want to use unreliable narration throughout the second act.
In the fan fiction I want to reimagine the side characters' inner lives away from the main plot.
In the graphic novel I want to alternate between two parallel timelines on facing pages.
In the screenplay adaptation I want to compress three acts into two for pacing reasons.
In the historical fiction I want to research the period rituals accurately enough to honor them.
In the romance novel I want to develop chemistry through sharp dialogue rather than narration.
In the children's book I want to introduce moral lessons gently through animal characters.
In the mystery I want to plant clues subtly across the early chapters before the reveal.
In the science fiction I want to ground the technology in plausible physics throughout.
In the noir film I want to use shadow and rain as recurring symbols of moral ambiguity.
In the anime adaptation I want to preserve the manga's distinctive pacing and panel rhythm.
In the crime drama I want to follow procedural authenticity in every interrogation scene.
In the literary fiction I want to focus on the protagonist's interior monologue more than action.
In the epistolary novel I want each letter to reveal a new angle of the central character.
In the audio drama I want to use ambient sound as the primary cue for setting and time.
neutral: In the kitchen I want to chop the onions thinly before adding them to the soup.
In the garden I want to water the tomatoes early before the afternoon heat sets in.
On the trail I want to keep a steady pace until reaching the second waypoint marker.
In the spreadsheet I want to sort the rows by date before applying the filter.
At the gym I want to warm up for ten minutes before starting the heavier lifts.
In the laundry I want to separate the colors from the whites before loading the machine.
In the workshop I want to sand the surface evenly before applying the first coat of stain.
On the piano I want to practice the scales slowly before attempting the arpeggios at tempo.
In the budget I want to allocate more for groceries before reducing the entertainment line.
At the grocery store I want to grab the produce first before heading to the dairy section.
In the dog's training I want to reinforce sit-and-stay before introducing the leash recall.
On the highway I want to stay in the right lane unless I need to pass a slower car.
In the toolbox I want to organize the wrenches by size before tackling the drawer of bits.
At the post office I want to weigh the package before stamping it for international shipping.
In the calendar I want to block off mornings for deep work before scheduling any meetings.
On the bookshelf I want to group the cookbooks together before reorganizing the rest of the shelves.
In the inbox I want to clear the urgent threads before starting on the newsletter queue.
At the bus stop I want to check the schedule once more before committing to the transfer.
In the basement I want to seal the gaps along the foundation before the rains return.
In the camera bag I want to repack the lenses by focal length before the next trip.
cooking_craft: In the kitchen I want to brown the butter slowly before adding the shallots.
In the saute pan I want to deglaze with white wine before reducing the cream.
In the dough I want to develop the gluten through long folds rather than kneading.
In the marinade I want to balance acid and oil before the meat goes in.
In the broth I want to skim the impurities every few minutes for clarity.
In the bechamel I want to whisk constantly until it thickens just before scorching.
In the risotto I want to add the stock one ladle at a time as the rice releases its starch.
In the pastry I want to keep the butter cold so the layers stay distinct in the oven.
In the stew I want to develop the fond on the bottom of the pot before deglazing.
In the souffle I want to fold the egg whites in carefully without deflating them.
In the curry I want to bloom the spices in oil before any liquid hits the pan.
In the sourdough I want to feed the starter on a strict 12-hour schedule for consistency.
In the consomme I want to clarify with an egg-white raft before straining through cheesecloth.
In the carbonara I want to temper the eggs slowly to avoid scrambling them in the pasta.
In the brining I want to use a salt-to-water ratio that suits the cut.
In the pickling I want to balance the vinegar acidity with sugar before pouring over the vegetables.
In the bread crumb I want to use stale day-old loaf so the texture stays uniform.
In the smoking I want to maintain a steady low temperature throughout the cook.
In the meringue I want to add sugar gradually after stiff peaks form for stability.
In the sauce reduction I want to scrape the fond and incorporate it into the final glaze.
music_craft: In the score I want to vary the dynamics through the recapitulation rather than simply repeat.
In the cadenza I want to leave space for the soloist to choose their own ornamentation.
In the orchestration I want to balance the strings with woodwinds rather than overpower.
In the harmony I want to delay the resolution until the final phrase for tension.
In the rhythm section I want to lock the kick and bass to the same subdivision.
In the fugue I want to stagger the entrances by exactly two measures.
In the modulation I want to pivot through the relative minor before settling.
In the chorale I want each voice to move stepwise wherever possible.
In the arrangement I want to thin the texture before the climax for contrast.
In the counterpoint I want to keep the lines independent enough to be heard separately.
In the sonata form I want to restate the second theme in the tonic during the recapitulation.
In the chord voicing I want to keep the third and seventh close together for color.
In the syncopation I want to avoid placing accents on the downbeat for the next bar.
In the suspension I want to prepare the dissonance on a strong beat.
In the trio section I want to lighten the instrumentation before the scherzo returns.
In the improvisation I want to develop one motif rather than chase new ones.
In the recording I want to capture the room sound rather than tight-mic everything.
In the mix I want to push the vocals slightly forward without overpowering the band.
In the mastering I want to preserve the dynamic range rather than chase loudness.
In the ensemble I want to phrase together as a single voice rather than sections.
programming_craft: In the function I want to memoize the recursion before adding the cache layer.
In the API I want to validate the request body before any database call.
In the migration I want to add the new column nullable first and backfill in a separate pass.
In the query I want to use a covering index to avoid touching the heap.
In the test I want to mock only at the boundary, not internal collaborators.
In the build I want to fail fast on lint errors before running the full test suite.
In the loop I want to break out as soon as the predicate is satisfied rather than scan everything.
In the data structure I want to choose a hash map for lookup over a sorted list for this size.
In the parser I want to use a recursive descent rather than regex for nested expressions.
In the cache I want to invalidate by tag rather than by individual key.
In the retry policy I want to apply exponential backoff with full jitter.
In the rollout I want to gate the new path behind a feature flag for the first week.
In the queue I want to use a dead-letter queue for messages that fail repeatedly.
In the observability layer I want to attach trace IDs at the request boundary.
In the deployment I want to canary the change to one percent before scaling out.
In the schema I want to make the column nullable until all writers have been updated.
In the authorization I want to enforce permission at the resource level rather than route level.
In the documentation I want to colocate the README with the code rather than a separate wiki.
In the linter config I want to opt into the strict rules rather than disable noisy ones.
In the rate limiter I want to use a token bucket per user rather than global.

declarative: Fiction develops character through specific detail.
A novel builds tension across multiple acts.
Stories give voice to inner conflict.
Literary fiction privileges character over plot.
The opening chapter establishes a novel's voice.
Symbolism and metaphor enrich literary fiction.
Narrative pacing varies between novels and short stories.
Dialogue can reveal character without explicit description.
A protagonist's interior monologue drives many novels.
Worldbuilding in speculative fiction requires consistency.
Plot arcs in fiction follow recognizable structural patterns.
The craft of storytelling has many traditions.
Genre fiction balances convention against innovation.
Theme often emerges through cumulative scene-work.
A short story's ending resonates with implied meaning.
Character development unfolds across multiple scenes.
Specificity of detail anchors a fictional world.
Tragic narratives often hinge on a turning point.
Voice in fiction is shaped by sentence-level choices.
The closing scene of a novel shapes how readers remember it.
fresh_craft: In the prologue I want to set the tone for the protagonist's eventual arc.
In the first act I want to establish the world before introducing the inciting incident.
In the dialogue I want to reveal character through subtext rather than exposition.
In the climax I want to pay off the foreshadowing planted earlier.
In the resolution I want to leave space for the reader to project meaning.
In the framing device I want to layer two timelines that converge in the final pages.
In the supporting cast I want to give each character a clear motivation that contrasts with the protagonist's.
In the antagonist's monologue I want to show genuine moral complexity rather than caricature.
In the third-act turn I want to upend a reader assumption that was set up in chapter two.
In the falling action I want to slow the pacing to let the emotional stakes land.
In the parallel subplot I want to mirror the protagonist's arc through a secondary character.
In the unreliable narration I want to make the reader question the protagonist's account in retrospect.
In the symbolic motif I want to recur through three pivotal scenes without becoming heavy-handed.
In the chapter break I want to leave a question unanswered that the next chapter complicates.
In the prose rhythm I want to vary sentence length to mirror emotional tempo.
In the world's history I want to seed implications without explicating them.
In the protagonist's ritual I want to convey character through repetition rather than description.
In the secondary antagonist I want to embody the temptation the protagonist must reject.
In the epistolary section I want to alternate two letter-writers' voices throughout.
In the final paragraph I want to recall an image from the opening for thematic closure.
first_person_no_help: I am writing a novel about isolation.
I write fiction in my spare time.
I have been working on this novel for over a year.
I love crafting fictional characters.
I plan to finish my novel by the end of next year.
I enjoy writing literary fiction more than genre.
I revise my fiction extensively before showing anyone.
I draft my scenes by hand before typing them up.
I outline my plots in detail before drafting.
I read widely to inform my fiction.
I focus on character interiority in my work.
I write every morning before my day job.
I am developing a novel set in a coastal town.
I prefer slow-paced literary novels.
I find dialogue easier to draft than exposition.
I structure my chapters with care for rhythm.
I treat my fiction as a long-term project rather than a sprint.

I write fiction because it lets me think through difficult emotions safely.
I aim to finish the manuscript before submitting to agents.
